abstract: "Uncertainty is an important element of predictions from clinical prediction
  models. Effective sample size translates uncertainty into a number of 'patients
  like you' that a prediction is effectively based on. In this article, we aim to
  explore the concept of effective sample size for the Cox model for prediction 
  of survival probabilities. It captures differences
  in uncertainty between individual patients who are more common in the model development
  data compared to patients who are less common. Surprisingly, effective sample size for predictions
  of specific patients early on in follow-up can be larger than the total development
  sample size. While this can be considered misleading in communication with patients,
  it does show that variance estimation of Cox model predictions is too optimistic
  early on in follow-up. In conclusion, effective sample size for the Cox model
  can inform about the uncertainty of individual predictions, but there are 
  unintuitive aspects, that make it unsuitable for communication with patients."
number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
---


## Introduction
Prognostic models with survival outcomes are abundant in disease areas such as oncology cardiology. Examples are the Melanoma Institute Australia models which provide survival predictions for multiple stages of melanoma [@pires2022; @varey2024] and the PREDIC model with aims to predict the benefit of treatment to survival probability [@predict]. Such models can provide value in clinical decision-making and patient counseling.

Uncertainty is an important element of predictions from prognostic models. If and how these models communicate uncertainty in the predicted survival probability to end-users varies a lot. Among the risk calculators of the Melanoma Institute Australia, the risk calculator for immunotherapy outcome makes no mention of prediction uncertainty [@pires2022]. The risk calculator for stage II melanoma survival does show 95% confidence intervals around all shown survival predictions [@varey2024].

At model development, at least we should be able to estimate the overall event rate with some reasonable certainty. For example, we may require a minimum number of events of 100 [@steyerbergboek]. Moreover, larger model complexity implies a need for larger numbers, as reflected in rules of thumb for the number of events per variable (EPV) such as 10 to 15 EPV for proportional hazards regression [@peduzzi1995;@harrellbook]. Such general rules have received criticism, because they don't work in all situations [@courvoisier2011]. New methods to calculate the correct sample size or EPV that take into consideration more aspects of the prediction model have been suggested [@riley2019]. More recently, focus has shifted to the stability of models and the uncertainty in predictions [@altman1989; @shen2016; @riley2023; @riley2023b; @barrenada2025; @riley2025].  

Effective sample size has been suggested as a promising summary measure of individual uncertainty in generalized linear models and machine learning models [@thomassen2024; @thomassen2025]. Effective sample size for a prediction for a patient can be interpreted as the number of patients with the same covariate values that the prediction would effectively be based on. For analysis of survival data, the concept of effective sample size was presented already by @kaplanmeier1958.). We recently illustrated that effective sample size provides a valuable and readily interpretable measure of uncertainty for survival curves in the presence of censoring [@hackmann2025]. Effective sample size needs further extension to survival predictions based on combinations of prognostic factors, as commonly provided by a Cox proportional hazards model in medical research. Such an extension might be used for the communication of uncertainty with individual patients, but also during model development, to investigate groups for which there may not be sufficient information on which to base predictions [@thomassen2025].

In this study, we aim to extend previously developed approaches [@thomassen2025; @hackmann2025] for effective sample size estimation to the Cox model. We hypothesize that effective sample size may be valuable for expressing individual uncertainty in predictions based on multiple prognostic factors, with appropriate handling of censored observations as for Kaplan-Meier curves. For effective sample size to be an intuitive measure of uncertainty, we have two requirements that we will investigate:

1. Effective sample size reflects the size of a sample with complete follow-up, accommodating censoring naturally

2. 2.	Effective sample size reflects the commonality or exceptionality of a patient profile within the development data

This paper continues with of a description of calculating the effective sample size based on the variance of predictions of the Cox model (section 2), followed by application of effective sample size calculations to Cox models in an illustrative data set (section 3). We explore effective sample size in more depth in simulations (section 4) and end with a discussion (section 5).

## Effective sample size for the Cox model {#sec-data-methods}
### Setting
We consider a survival prediction for a specific new patient named ('Sam') at a predetermined time $t$ using a Cox model. This is a personalized survival prediction $\hat S(t | \mathbf{x}_{Sam}) = \hat S_{Sam}(t)$, based on their covariate values $\mathbf{x}_{Sam}$. We take as a reference the situation where we have a number $N_*$ of patients that all have the same covariate values $\mathbf{x}_{Sam}$ as Sam. In this virtual sample, we can observe the mean outcome $\bar S_*(t)$ at time $t$. With $\bar S_*(t) = \hat S_{Sam}(t)$, we can calculate the number $N_*$ as the number of patients required to obtain the same certainty in our estimation of $\bar S_*(t)$, as the model estimation of $\hat S_{Sam}(t)$. This number $N_*$ is the effective sample size.

### Definition
If we assume that all patients with the same covariate values have the same variance, we can estimate $N_*$ using:
$$
N_* := \frac{V(\bar S_*(t))}{V(\hat S_{Sam}(t))},
$${#eq-n_eff}
where $V(\bar S_*(t)) = \bar S_*(t)\left(1-\bar S_*(t)\right)$ is the binomial variance and $V(\hat S_{Sam}(t))$ is the variance of the Cox model estimate for Sam. For the variance of the Cox model, different estimation procedures of the variance can be used. We will use a standard definition for the Cox model variance based on the delta method [@tsiatis1981; @andersen1993], which is implemented in frequently used software for survival analysis [@package-survival; @mstate2011].  The variance of a Cox model prediction for Sam is:

$$
V(\hat S_{Sam}(t)) = \hat V\left( \hat S(t | \mathbf{x}_{Sam})\right)\biggr\rvert_{\beta} + \left( \frac{\partial\hat S}{\partial\beta}\right)\Biggr\rvert^\intercal_{\beta = \hat \beta} \hat V(\hat \beta) \left( \frac{\partial\hat S}{\partial\beta}\right)\Biggr\rvert_{\beta = \hat \beta},
$${#eq-cox_var}
where $\hat V\left( \hat S(t| \mathbf{x}_{new})\right)\biggr\rvert_{\beta}$ is the variance of the baseline hazard under correct coefficient estimation and $\left( \frac{\partial\hat S}{\partial\beta}\right)\Biggr\rvert^\intercal_{\beta = \hat \beta} \hat V(\hat \beta) \left( \frac{\partial\hat S}{\partial\beta}\right)\Biggr\rvert_{\beta = \hat \beta}$ is the variance caused by misestimation of the coefficients. The variance of the baseline hazard can be estimated using:
$$
\hat{S}^2(t| \mathbf{x}_{new}) \mathrm{exp}(2\hat{\mathbf{\beta}}^\top\mathbf{x}_{Sam}) \left( \sum_{i=1}^l \frac{1}{\left\{ \sum_{j\in R_i} \mathrm{exp}(\hat{\mathbf{\beta}}^\top \mathbf{x}_{j}) \right\}^2} \right),
$${#eq-cox_var_basehaz}
which is the Aalen formula for the variance of the baseline survival function based on the risk-weighted number at risk, muliplied by the squared relative risk of our new patient Sam. 

The uncertainty from the estimation of the coefficient depends on the variance of the coefficients and the gradient of the survival function w.r.t. the coefficients. The variance of the coefficients $\hat V(\hat\beta)$, is the inverse Fisher information matrix $\mathcal{I}(\beta)^{-1}$. Finally, we can estimate the gradient of the survival function with respect to the coefficient by:
$$
\frac{\partial\hat S(t| \mathbf{x}_{new})}{\partial\beta} = \mathrm{exp}(\hat{\mathbf{\beta}}^\top \mathbf{x}_{new}) \mathbf{w},
$${#eq-cox_var_grad}
where $\mathbf{w}$ is a vector of partial derivatives, with $\mathbf{w}_p$ the partial derivative of the survival function in the direction of $\beta_p$, with:

$$
w_p = \sum_{i=1}^l \frac{1}{\sum_{j\in R_i}\mathrm{exp}(\hat{\mathbf{\beta}}^\top \mathbf{x}_{j})} \left\{ \frac{\sum_{j\in R_i} x_{j,p}\mathrm{exp}(\hat\beta^\top \mathbf{x}_j)}{ \sum_{j\in R_i} \mathrm{exp}(\hat{\mathbf{\beta}}^\top \mathbf{x}_{j})} - x_{new,p} \right\}.
$${#eq-cox_var_wp}
We can see in the definition of $\mathbf{w}_p$ that the variance, and therefore effective sample size, depends on the difference between our patient's new covariate $x_{Sam, p}$ and the distribution of $x_p$ in the risk set.

Note that other methods exist for calculating the variance, such as the von Mises expansion [@vandervaart1998], implemented in [@riskRegression]. Any method works for estimating effective sample size using @eq-n_eff, however the estimate may be different, because of differences in estimated variance.

## Application to colon cancer data
### Data description
We used data the `colon` data that is available within the `survival` package in `R` [@R; @package-survival]. This data set contains results from a trial of adjuvant levimasole or levimasole+5-fluorouracil compared to observation only in 929 patients [@laurie1989; @moertel1995]. Mortality was the primary outcome, which was observed in 452 of 929 patients. Four baseline prognostic factors were considered in this study, treatment and adhesion of the tumor as categorical variables and age and number of positive lymph nodes as continuous variables. The distribution of these variables in the data can be seen in Supplement 2.

### Illustration with one predictor
Effective sample sizes for Cox model predictions were much higher than those of stratified Kaplan-Meier curves for early time points. The difference in effective sample size between Cox model predictions and Kaplan-Meier decreased over time with increasing number of events.

cox_adhere_0 <- survfit_n( survfit(cox_adhere, newdata=data.frame(adhere=0)), cox=cox_adhere)
cox_adhere_1 <- survfit_n( survfit(cox_adhere, newdata=data.frame(adhere=1)), cox=cox_adhere)

linetype_values <- c("Cox" = "solid", "Kaplan-Meier" = "dashed")

# Create the plots
plot1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = cox_rx_0$time, y = cox_rx_0$surv, color = "Obs", linetype = "Cox"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = cox_rx_1$time, y = cox_rx_1$surv, color = "Lev", linetype = "Cox"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = cox_rx_2$time, y = cox_rx_2$surv, color = "Lev+5FU", linetype = "Cox"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = km_rx_0$time, y = km_rx_0$surv, color = "Obs", linetype = "Kaplan-Meier"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = km_rx_1$time, y = km_rx_1$surv, color = "Lev", linetype = "Kaplan-Meier"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = km_rx_2$time, y = km_rx_2$surv, color = "Lev+5FU", linetype = "Kaplan-Meier"), lwd = 1.3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = color_values) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = linetype_values) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Model based on treatment", limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = NULL, labels = NULL) +
  ggtitle("Kaplan-Meier & Cox Survival Probability") +
  theme(legend.position = "none") # Remove legend from individual plot

plot2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = cox_rx_0$time, y = cox_rx_0$n.eff, color = "Obs", linetype = "Cox"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = cox_rx_1$time, y = cox_rx_1$n.eff, color = "Lev", linetype = "Cox"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = cox_rx_2$time, y = cox_rx_2$n.eff, color = "Lev+5FU", linetype = "Cox"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = km_rx_0$time, y = km_rx_0$n.eff, color = "Obs", linetype = "Kaplan-Meier"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = km_rx_1$time, y = km_rx_1$n.eff, color = "Lev", linetype = "Kaplan-Meier"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = km_rx_2$time, y = km_rx_2$n.eff, color = "Lev+5FU", linetype = "Kaplan-Meier"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 929, linetype = "dotted", color = "black", linewidth = 0.6) +

  scale_linetype_manual(values = linetype_values) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_y_continuous(name = NULL, limits = c(0, 1000)) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = NULL, labels = NULL) +
  ggtitle("Effective Sample Size") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")  # Remove legend from individual plot

plot3 <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = cox_adhere_0$time, y = cox_adhere_0$surv, color = "No Adhesion", linetype = "Cox"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = cox_adhere_1$time, y = cox_adhere_1$surv, color = "Adhesion", linetype = "Cox"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = km_adhere_0$time, y = km_adhere_0$surv, color = "No Adhesion", linetype = "Kaplan-Meier"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = km_adhere_1$time, y = km_adhere_1$surv, color = "Adhesion", linetype = "Kaplan-Meier"), lwd = 1.3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = color_values) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = linetype_values) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Model based on adhesion", limits = c(0, 1)) +  # Remove y-axis name and tick labels
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Time (days)") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")  # Remove legend from individual plot

plot4 <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = cox_adhere_0$time, y = cox_adhere_0$n.eff, color = "No Adhesion", linetype = "Cox"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = cox_adhere_1$time, y = cox_adhere_1$n.eff, color = "Adhesion", linetype = "Cox"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = km_adhere_0$time, y = km_adhere_0$n.eff, color = "No Adhesion", linetype = "Kaplan-Meier"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_line(aes(x = km_adhere_1$time, y = km_adhere_1$n.eff, color = "Adhesion", linetype = "Kaplan-Meier"), lwd = 1.3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 929, linetype = "dotted", color = "black", linewidth = 0.6) +
  scale_color_manual(values = color_values) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = linetype_values) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_y_continuous(name = NULL, limits = c(0, 1000)) +  # Remove y-axis name and tick labels
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Time (days)") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")  # Remove legend from individual plot

# Combine the plots using patchwork and add a single legend on the right
combined_plot <- (plot1 / plot3 | plot2 / plot4) +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") &
  theme(legend.position = "right")

# Print the combined plot
print(combined_plot)

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
color_values <- c("Obs"= "#2E7691", "Kaplan-Meier" = "#C2666B", "Cox"= "#37293F", "Lev+5FU" = "#c6aa2c", "Adhesion" = "#9f84af")
linetype_values <- c("Prediction" = "solid", "95% CI" = "dashed")
plotx <- ggplot() +
  # Cox curves
  geom_line(aes(x = cox_rx_2$time, y = cox_rx_2$surv, color = "Cox", linetype = "Prediction"),
            lwd = 1.3, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_line(aes(x = cox_rx_2$time, y = cox_rx_2$upper, color = "Cox", linetype = "95% CI"),
            lwd = 1.3, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_line(aes(x = cox_rx_2$time, y = cox_rx_2$lower, color = "Cox", linetype = "95% CI"),
            lwd = 1.3, alpha = 0.5) +

  # Kaplan–Meier curves
  geom_line(aes(x = km_rx_2$time, y = km_rx_2$surv, color = "Kaplan-Meier", linetype = "Prediction"),
            lwd = 1.3, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_line(aes(x = km_rx_2$time, y = km_rx_2$upper, color = "Kaplan-Meier", linetype = "95% CI"),
            lwd = 1.3, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_line(aes(x = km_rx_2$time, y = km_rx_2$lower, color = "Kaplan-Meier", linetype = "95% CI"),
            lwd = 1.3, alpha = 0.5) +

  # Double arrow for Kaplan–Meier at t = 480
  geom_segment(
    {
      i <- which.max(ifelse(km_rx_2$time < 480, km_rx_2$time, -Inf))
      aes(x = km_rx_2$time[i], xend = km_rx_2$time[i],
          y = km_rx_2$lower[i], yend = km_rx_2$upper[i])
    },
    color = color_values["Kaplan-Meier"],
    linewidth = 1.2,
    arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm"), ends = "both", type = "closed")
  ) +

  # Double arrow for Cox at t = 520
  geom_segment(
    {
      j <- which.max(ifelse(cox_rx_2$time < 520, cox_rx_2$time, -Inf))
      aes(x = cox_rx_2$time[j], xend = cox_rx_2$time[j],
          y = cox_rx_2$lower[j], yend = cox_rx_2$upper[j])
    },
    color = color_values["Cox"],
    linewidth = 1.2,
    arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm"), ends = "both", type = "closed")
  ) +

  scale_color_manual(values = color_values) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = linetype_values) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Survival probability") +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Time (days)") +

@fig-category shows that the effective sample size for the patients with `Lev+5FU` treatment have an effective sample size that is higher than the total sample size of 929 early on in follow-up. The category with this higher effective sample size has a HR below 1 compared to both other categories. We can check to see if higher effective sample size does translate to narrower confidence intervals by comparing the confidence interval of the Cox model prediction for a patient with `Lev+5FU` treatment to the confidence interval of the Kaplan-Meier curve for this group. In @fig-compare-ci we do indeed see that the confidence interval of the Cox model prediction is narrower than that of the Kaplan-Meier curve, especially early on in follow-up.  

For Cox models based on continuous variables, effective sample size can vary highly depending on covariate values. In @fig-continuous we note that for patients with common (median) age, the effective sample size stayed close to the total number of patients in the population until censoring starts to impact effective sample size. For patients with uncommon (75th percentile) or rare (99th percentile) ages, the effective sample size decreased very rapidly during early follow-up. For all three example patients, their survival predictions (dotted lines) were very similar, since the prognostic effect of age was very small.

For the number of nodes, the common value is 2 positive nodes (median), the uncommon is 5 positive nodes (75th percentile) and the rare value is 17 (99th percentile). Patients with 2 nodes have an effective sample size of more than 1000 early on in follow-up, higher than the total sample size of 929. Additionally, for a patient with 17 nodes, the effective sample size of their prediction increases over time as their survival probability approaches 0.

### Effective sample size in multivariable Cox models
A survival prediction from a Cox model is often presented as the survival probability at specific times points $t$. It may not be relevant that effective sample size can reach unintuitive values higher than the development sample size at early follow-up times, if it is intuitively interpretable at the time point of interest. We fit four Cox models, each time adding a predictor: (A) Surv(time, status) ~ rx; (B) Surv(time, status) ~ rx + adhere; (C) Surv(time, status) ~ rx + adhere + nodes; (D) Surv(time, status) ~ rx + adhere + nodes + age. 


::: {#cell-fig-distribution-dev .cell}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
source("code/compile.R")

year <- 5
var1 <- "rx"; var2 <- "rx + adhere"; var3 <- "rx + adhere + nodes"; var4 <- "rx + adhere + nodes + age"
leg1 <- "Tx"; leg2 <- "Tx + Adh"; leg3 <- "Tx + Adh + Nodes"; leg4 <- "Tx + Adh + Nodes + Age"


# Single impute the nodes data
colon_df$nodes <- mice::complete(mice::mice(colon_df, m = 1, print = FALSE))$nodes

# Fit all cox models
cox_model1 <- coxph(as.formula(paste0( "Surv( time, status) ~ ", var1)), data = colon_df)
cox_model2 <- coxph(as.formula(paste0( "Surv( time, status) ~ ", var2)), data = colon_df)
cox_model3 <- coxph(as.formula(paste0( "Surv( time, status) ~ ", var3)), data = colon_df)
cox_model4 <- coxph(as.formula(paste0( "Surv( time, status) ~ ", var4)), data = colon_df)

# Create prediction df
out_df1 = out_df2 = out_df3 = out_df4 <- colon_df |>
  select( rx, age, adhere, nodes )
out_df1$model <- paste0( "(A) Surv( time, status) ~ ", var1)
out_df2$model <- paste0( "(B) Surv( time, status) ~ ", var2)
out_df3$model <- paste0( "(C) Surv( time, status) ~ ", var3)
out_df4$model <- paste0( "(D) Surv( time, status) ~ ", var4)


# Create SF object for correct time
sf1 <- summary( survfit(cox_model1, newdata = out_df1), time = year*365.25 )
sf2 <- summary( survfit(cox_model2, newdata = out_df2), time = year*365.25 )
sf3 <- summary( survfit(cox_model3, newdata = out_df3), time = year*365.25 )
sf4 <- summary( survfit(cox_model4, newdata = out_df4), time = year*365.25 )

# Save survival probabilities
out_df1$surv <- c(sf1$surv)
out_df2$surv <- c(sf2$surv)
out_df3$surv <- c(sf3$surv)
out_df4$surv <- c(sf4$surv)

# Calculate the effective sample size for the 5-year prediction
out_df1$n.eff <- (1-out_df1$surv)*out_df1$surv/(c(sf1$std.err)^2)
out_df2$n.eff <- (1-out_df2$surv)*out_df2$surv/(c(sf2$std.err)^2)
out_df3$n.eff <- (1-out_df3$surv)*out_df3$surv/(c(sf3$std.err)^2)
out_df4$n.eff <- (1-out_df4$surv)*out_df4$surv/(c(sf4$std.err)^2)

out_df <- rbind(out_df1, out_df2, out_df3, out_df4)
out_df$model <- factor(out_df$model, levels = c(paste0( "(A) Surv( time, status) ~ ", var1), 
                                                paste0( "(B) Surv( time, status) ~ ", var2),  
                                                paste0( "(C) Surv( time, status) ~ ", var3), 
                                                paste0( "(D) Surv( time, status) ~ ", var4)))

# Create a scatter plot
colors <- c("#37293F", "#2E7691",  "#9f84af",  "#c6aa2c")
names(colors) <- c(paste0( "(A) Surv( time, status) ~ ", var1), 
                   paste0( "(B) Surv( time, status) ~ ", var2),  
                   paste0( "(C) Surv( time, status) ~ ", var3), 
                   paste0( "(D) Surv( time, status) ~ ", var4))

plot <- ggplot(out_df) +
  geom_point( aes(x = surv, y = n.eff, color = model), data = out_df, alpha = 0.2, size = 2) +
  facet_wrap( ~model ) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Predicted 5-Year Survival Probability", limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Effective Sample Size", limits = c(0, 500)) +
  #ggtitle("Changes distribution of Eff. N. between models") +
  labs( color = "Legend") +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = colors
  ) +
  #theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none") + 
  guides(colour = guide_legend(override.aes = list(alpha = 1)))

# Print the plot
plot
save(plot, file = "images/fig-distribution.RData" )
rm( list = ls())

The second model adds adhesion, the third model adds nodes and the fourth model adds age It can be seen that the effective sample size does not decrease for all patients with increasing variables, but it does for patients with rare covariate values.](manuscript_files/figure-html/fig-distribution-1.png){#fig-distribution width=960}

In @fig-distribution we can see the predicted survival probabilities plotted against effective sample sizes for each of the patients in the data based on the Cox models. The addition of adhesion to a model based only on treatment adds some changes in predicted probability, but decreases the effective sample size for part of the patients substantially. Similarly, the addition of nodes decreases the effective sample size for many patients, while also increasing the spread in predicted survival outcomes. In contrast, the addition of age to this model shows very little increase in prediction spread while there is much more heterogeneity in effective sample sizes. Indeed, `age` was a relatively weak predictor (univariate Nagelkerke $R^2$ 0.025\%) of survival outcome, whereas `nodes` was a much stronger predictor (univariate Nagelkerke $R^2$ 6.1\%).

### Is effective sample size intuitive?
Effective sample size for Cox model predictions does seem to reflect size of a hypothetical sample of $N_{eff}$ patients at later follow-up times. In @fig-category we see that effective sample size decreases towards the Kaplan-Meier effective sample size with increasing follow-up and then decrease due to censoring at a similar time. In @fig-distribution, we can also see that the addition of variables to the Cox model increases the spread in effective sample sizes, reflecting the heterogeneity in available information for patients with different covariate values.

Effective sample size for the Cox model does show intuitive aspects with respect to the differences between effective sample size for patients with uncommon and common covariate values. Patients with exceptional covariate values have their effective sample size reduced very quickly after the start of follow-up. Patients with common covariate values, such as the sample mean/median as seen in @fig-continuous, have effective sample sizes very close to the complete development sample size in the absence of censoring.

However, in @fig-category we can see that the effective sample size for at least one of the categories of patients has an effective sample size larger than the total development sample size. This does not align with an intuitive understanding of effective sample size a this hypothetical sample of $N_{eff}$ patients like you that a prediction is based on, since we know for certain that there were fewer.
Additionally, the observation in @fig-continuous that effective sample size can increase later in follow-up is unintuitive, since it does not make sense that after uncertainty of predictions decreases after patients were censored.


## Investigation of effective sample size in simulations
### Simulation setting
We ran simulations with the aim of understanding when effective sample size would be higher than the total sample size. For this purpose, survival times were simulated first without any censoring and with only one binary predictor variable, reflecting survival in men and women. A hazard ratio of 2 for men compared to women was chosen for illustration. Sample size in the simulation was set to 10,000 (5,000 men and 5,000 women) to ensure that the hazard ratio could be accurately estimated between the two groups. Baseline hazard for the simulation was set to a constant of 1, and maximum follow-up was set to 10 years.

### Simulation
Cox model and Kaplan-Meier survival estimates are similar for both groups. This is expected, since the data were generated with proportional hazards (HR of $2$ for 5,000 men compared to 5,000 women) for one categorical predictor (sex). The Kaplan-Meier effective sample sizes for both groups remain constant at $5,000$, the number of observations in each of the groups because there is no censoring. The Cox model effective sample size begins at $15,000$ for women (the group with lower hazard) and at $7,500$ for men (the group with higher hazard). Additionally, effective sample size for men increases after $t=7.5$.

::: {#cell-fig-sim-dev .cell}

```{.r .cell-code .hidden}
source("code/compile.R")
require(surveillance)

# Seed for consistency
set.seed(1923)

scale = c(10)
shape = c(1) # shape 1 means exponential
maxtime = 100 # maxtime
hr = 2 # hazard ratio
n = 5000 # number per group

events <- simsurv( dist="weibull", 
                   gammas=shape, 
                   lambdas=scale[1]^(-shape), 
                   x=data.frame(tx = c(rep(0, n), rep(1, n))),
                   betas = c("tx" = log(hr)),
                   maxt=maxtime)
events$tx = c(rep(0, n), rep(1, n))
events$eventtime[8885] <- 2.997671  # handmatig ties weghalen
events$eventtime[6298] <- 5.668818  # werkt omdat seed vast staats
time <- sort(unique(events$eventtime))
time0 <- sort(unique(events[events$tx == 0, ]$eventtime))
time1 <- sort(unique(events[events$tx == 1, ]$eventtime))

# Fit the models
sf0 <- survfit(Surv(eventtime, status) ~ 1, data = events[events$tx == 0,])
sf1 <- survfit(Surv(eventtime, status) ~ 1, data = events[events$tx == 1,])
cox <- coxph(Surv(eventtime, status) ~ tx, data = events)

# Calculate effective N and stuff
sf_n0 <- survfit_n(sf0) |> sf_to_df(time = time0)
sf_n1 <- survfit_n(sf1) |> sf_to_df(time = time1)
cox_n0 <- survfit_n(survfit(cox, newdata = data.frame(tx = 0)), cox, coef=T, chaz=T)
cox_n1 <- survfit_n(survfit(cox, newdata = data.frame(tx = 1)), cox, coef=T, chaz=T)

cox_n0 <- sf_to_df(cox_n0, time = time)
cox_n1 <- sf_to_df(cox_n1, time = time)

colors <- c("1" = "#37293F", "3" = "#2E7691",  "2" = "#9f84af", "4" = "#c6aa2c")
linetype <- c("1" = "solid", "2" = "dashed")

  geom_line(aes(x = time1, y = sf_n1$n.eff, color = "4"), size = 1.2) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Time", limits = c(0, 15)) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Effective Sample Size", limits = c(0, 15000)) +
  ggtitle("Effective Sample Size of Coxph and Survfit") +
  labs( color = "Legend") +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = colors,
    labels = c("Cox Women", "Cox Men", "KM Women", "KM Men")
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "right") + 
  guides(colour = guide_legend(override.aes = list(alpha = 1)))


# plot the two kaplan-meiers overlapping in one plot and the cox survival predictions for the 0 an 1 treatment patients
plot2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = time0, y = sf_n0$surv, color = "3"), size = 1.2) +
  geom_line(aes(x = time1, y = sf_n1$surv, color = "4"), size = 1.2) +
  geom_line(aes(x = time, y = cox_n0$surv, color = "1"), size = 1.2) +
  geom_line(aes(x = time, y = cox_n1$surv, color = "2"), size = 1.2) +

# Combine the plots using patchwork and add a single legend on the right

We investigate both of these unexpected behaviours of effective sample size in more detail by looking at the variance of the Cox model predictions. We split the variance of the Cox model prediction into the variance caused by the estimation of the baseline hazard (first term of r.h.s. sum (@eq-cox_var)) and the variance caused by the inaccurate estimation of the coefficient and its effect on the survival outcome (second term of r.h.s. sum (@eq-cox_var)). 

In @fig-variance-inc (l.h.s.), the contribution to the total variance of the baseline hazard estimation as a percentage of total variance is shown. We find that early on in follow-up the group with a lower hazard has an effective sample size which is higher than the total sample size. This is explained by the variance of the estimation of the baseline hazard, since this is the source of nearly all of the variance early on in follow-up (@fig-variance-inc). We can also look at the increment, or increase in standard error of the Cox model estimate with each subsequent event (@fig-variance-inc, r.h.s.). Here we see that the incremental increase in variance due to coefficient estimation for men is about the same as that of the baseline hazard after $t=5$. For women, it is about the same up to $t=7.5$, after which the increment of the coefficient variance becomes a lot smaller than that of the baseline hazard. This coincides with the inflection point where the percentage contribution of the baseline hazard begins to increase again.

#### Heuristic explanation: Baseline hazard estimation
The baseline hazard for the Cox model at time $t_i$ with $\delta_i$ events is $\frac{\delta_i}{\sum_{j\in R_i}\exp(\beta'X_j)}$, where the denominator is the risk-weighted number at risk. This risk-weighted number at risk is the implicit sample size used for the baseline hazard estimation. A heuristic explanation for the effective sample sizes observed near $t=0$ in @fig-sim would be the following. Assuming the hazard ratio was correctly estimated by the model and there has been a single death in 10,000 patients. The risk-weighted number at risk in the data is $5,000\cdot2 + 5,000\cdot 1 = 15,000$. If you are a woman, your risk of being this first death are $\frac{1}{15,000}$, giving us an effective sample size of 15,000. If you are a man, your risk of being this first death are $\frac{2}{15,000} = \frac{1}{7,500}$, giving us an effective sample size of 7,500. 

We can check this explanation in our simulated data. On the l.h.s. of @fig-variance-inc, we can see that indeed most of the contribution to the variance early on in follow-up is from the baseline hazard estimation. Additionally, with the Cox-model estimated HR of 1.98, the expected effective sample sizes from our explanation would be 14910 for women and 7523 for men. The estimated effective sample sizes are 14907 and 7523 respectively, where the small difference for women can be explained by the contribution of the coefficient estimation. Details can be read in Supplement 6.

#### Heuristic explanation: Coefficient contribution
In @fig-variance-inc, we can see that the contribution of the coefficient estimation to the total variance is somewhat similar between the two groups in relation to the baseline hazard contribution. At $t=7.5$ we can clearly see a divergence of this pattern, where the contribution of the coefficient tot the total variance for women drops off substantially. Using the formula from @eq-cox_var_wp we can explain this.

At the time of the first event, the gradient (@eq-cox_var_grad) for both patients is $\frac{2}{45000}$ and the variance of the coefficient is constant. Therefore the uncertainty from possibly wrongly estimating the coefficient is the same for both groups. Calculating the absolute number at later time points is more difficult, because the gradient is a cumulative sum over all event times. We can look at the newest term of this sum in the gradient at time $t_i$. We choose $t=6.931$, which is the exact time where $S_{women}(6.931)=0.5$ and $S_{men}(6.931)=0.25$ (Supplement 7). If an event occurs at this time, the expected new addition to the cumulative sum of the gradient is $\frac{1}{10000}$ for women and $-\frac{2}{10000}$ for men because of the drift in the number of patients at risk from each of the groups. 

\textbf{Maybe add additional stuff to explain why Neff can go up with low survival - talk to Jelle?} 
Current idea: It has to do with the fact that the coefficient hazard decreases again for certain groups, in large part because of population shift - while $S(t)(1-S(t))$ going to 0 more slowly. So the variance does not increase as quickly as we would expect, as seen in the coefficient contribution for men in @fig-variance-inc.

## Discussion
Effective sample size behaves in a complicated way for the Cox proportional hazards model. We will check our findings according to the criteria set in the introduction.

1. Effective sample size reflects the size of a sample with complete follow-up, accommodating censoring naturally.
In some respects, effective sample size for the Cox model does align with this criterium. The effective sample size starts out higher and decreases with longer follow-up. Without censoring, it decreases towards the effective sample size of a Kaplan-Meier estimate in a Cox model with one categorical variable. After censoring, effective sample size also decreases to reflect this. However, effective sample size can also increase with longer follow-up for patients with high hazards. Sample size of a hypothetical study population tends not to increase with time, so this is unintuitive behaviour.

2. Effective sample size reflects the commonality or exceptionality of a patient profile within the development data.
We believe this criterium is met as shown in @fig-continuous. Patients with common profiles, such as the median age, can have an effective sample size equal to the development sample size for a long time, while patients with exceptional age (99th percentile) see a rapid decrease in their effective sample size after only a few events in the development data.

3. Effective sample size is not larger than the total sample size of the development data.
Unfortunately, this criterium is violated in many cases early on in the follow-up. This in unintuitive and can lead to misunderstanding when communicated with patients. While this can be explained by the variance of the estimation of the baseline hazard, it does complicate communication early on in follow-up.

While effective sample size for the Cox model does not seem intuitive for communicating uncertainty, it can help understanding the uncertainty in individual survival predictions. The expression of uncertainty as an absolute number of patients allows detailed discussion about the uncertainty of Cox model predictions. We will discuss the reason for the unintuitive effective sample size numbers and how this also affects 95% prediction intervals.

### Unintuitive aspects of effective sample size
Early on in follow-up, effective sample size can be larger than the total sample size. This may seem unreasonable for the communication of uncertainty in the prediction. This unnatural phenomenon is hidden when showing a confidence interval. We hypothesize that there are four factors that contribute to the effective sample size of Cox model predictions that make it less intuitive:
1. Normality and linearity assumptions
2. Issues with variance estimation when the event probability is very high or low
3. Borrowing information
4. Estimation of the baseline hazard

#### Normality and linearity assumptions
Already in effective sample size for linear regression models, we can see the first factor that contributes to higher effective sample sizes than we would expect. In linear regression we assume a linear effect an normally distributed covariates. Effective sample size in linear regression is bounded by the total sample size [@thomassen2024]. For prediction from a linear regression model with multiple variable, a patient with exactly the population mean covariate values will have exactly the total development sample as effective sample size (Supplement 2). Does it make intuitive sense that we are as certain of the event probability for such a patient, based on a model with a heterogeneous population, as we would have been if we observed this outcome in a sample of this number of exactly the same patient?

#### Issues with variance estimation when the event probability is very high or low
When the outcome variance is assumed to be according to a binomial distribution, with very high or very low event probabilities, $p(1-p)$ becomes very small. This small outcome variance can lead to inflated effective sample sizes for patients with very low or high event probabilities. This can be observed in logistic regression [@thomassen2024] and Cox models for high and low event probabilities (@fig-distribution). However, in Cox models effective sample size can also increase over time before the event rate becomes very low (@fig-sim).

#### Borrowing information
Effective sample size can be interpreted as the number of patients like you that your prediction is based on. Modeling assumptions mean that we borrow information from other groups of patients as well. You might expect your effective sample size to be bound by the number of patients with your gender for a model based on age and gender. This is the case for linear regression, but not for logistic regression (Supplement 4). When adding an interaction term between the age and gender in the logistic regression model, effective sample size is lower than the number of patients in your subgroup again. This implies that the effective sample size being larger than your subset is caused by a combination of the model borrowing information from the other subgroups for the estimation of the continuous variable coefficient, together with an effect from the link function.

#### Estimation of the baseline hazard
The estimation of the baseline hazard contributes most to the effective sample size early on in follow-up. This is because the coefficients have a relative effect on the baseline hazard and a relative effect on a very small hazard is a very small effect of the coefficient. The estimation of the baseline hazard has a big impact on the effective sample size and is a primary contributor to the unintuitive effective sample size early on in follow-up. Because the baseline hazard is estimated using the risk-weighted number at risk based on all patients, it is a very stable estimate. The assumption that there is a common baseline hazard and proportional covariate effects cause this certainty.

### Cox model variance is too low
The factors described above affect the effective sample size, but they do so through the variance of a Cox model prediction. When the effective sample size could be considered unreasonably large, the variance and resulting 95% confidence interval of the prediction could also be considered unreasonably small. Methods for simultaneous confidence bands for Cox model predictions exist [@lin1994], including software implementation [@riskRegression]. However, these are not common and most models simply connect the dots of pointwise confidence intervals [@sachs2022]. For models predicting a binary outcome, there is no destinction between a confidence and prediction interval, any additional uncertainty in predicting for new patients is captured in the aleatory uncertainty of a risk prediction [@steyerbergboek].

### Effective sample size for the communication of uncertainty
Effective sample size can be a tool for the communication of uncertainty. The interpretation helps with putting the certainty estimate into context. At later follow-up times, effective sample size shows reasonable estimates compared to the total sample size. It can be valuable to use effective sample size in the communication of uncertainty. However, for the Cox model specifically, effective sample size can be unreasonably high early on in follow-up. This is a problem for communication with patients, since it can lead to misunderstandings and overconfidence in the prediction. Therefore we would advise caution when using it to communicate uncertainty to patients.


### Effective sample size in model development
In @fig-distribution, we have shown that the addition of covariates to a Cox model has different impacts on the spread of predictions and effective sample sizes. This also shows that a balance must be struck between bias and variance to make predictions for individual patients. The addition of age to the treatment-only model, for example, only changed the predictions slightly, but decreased the effective sample size for older and younger patients substantially. This trade-off must be considered when developing individual prediction models. Effective sample size provides insight into such trade-offs. We only recommend using effective sample size in addition to other measures to indicate model performance such as the improvement in model fit, as expressed with R^2 values or Brier score.

Additionally, in the full model in @fig-distribution, certain patients have extremely low effective sample sizes. Such low effective sample sizes can lead to subsets of patients that are excluded from the use of the model, because their predictions are too uncertain to be relied upon. Another option would be windsorizing the covariates or the patient values to values that are more common and therefore lead to more certain predictions.

## Conclusion
Effective sample size for the Cox model is an complicated measure for the uncertainty of individual survival predictions. Early on in follow-up, the survival probability and uncertainty are dominated by the estimated baseline hazard, such that the effective sample size for specific patient profiles can be higher than the total sample size. This reflects that the Cox model prediction variance is lower than the outcome variance in a sample of $N_{eff}$ patients with the same covariate values with complete follow-up.

Hence, effective sample size can be misleading for short follow-up times. Effective sample size for Cox model predictions is more meaningful for late follow-up times. Further research is needed into the understanding of measures for uncertainty before communication with effective sample size is implemented in clinical practice.
